extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The **physical twin system** under study is the **serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes**, designed for **precision machining operations**. This system operates within a **highly dynamic, multi-physics environment**, emphasizing **continuous, real-time monitoring and adaptive control** to optimize joint friction identification and improve motion accuracy through closed-loop feedback mechanisms. The operational domain prioritizes **high-precision robotic manipulation** with minimal latency, leveraging cyber-physical integration for real-time adjustments in industrial automation contexts.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides **visualization services** via a **dashboard** for real-time monitoring of joint friction and system performance. Its core service is **continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of friction loss** through aggregated data traces, enabling **detection and analysis** of anomalies in the robot’s operational behavior. The system supports **traceability** by linking data traces back to their processing components (e.g., simulation or data calculation models) and input traces, ensuring **transitive traceability** for computational dependencies. The Digital Twin is **purpose-driven**, designed to address specific use cases (e.g., machining) rather than being data-centric.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The Digital Twin system relies on the following **tooling and enablers**, as referenced in the documents:
  - **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) Methodologies**:
    - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**: Tools like **SysML** (for component & connector modeling, [17]), **AADL** ([24]), or **MontiArc** ([25]) to describe functional/logical architectures and behavior (e.g., state machines in SysML v1.7).
    - **Methodologies**: **RFLP** (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT** ([18]–[22]) for handling system complexity across abstraction layers.
  - **Digital Shadow Implementation**:
    - **Data Collection**: Active components interpret and process incoming data traces from physical systems (e.g., sensors in injection molding, [64]).
    - **Processing Components**: Use **data calculation models** or **simulation engines** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink [28]) to compute higher-level abstractions from input traces, ensuring traceability (as per [63]).
    - **Reference Models**: UML Class Diagrams (e.g., Fig. 1) define domain concepts and conformance relations for digital shadow concretizations (e.g., mapping reference classes to concrete elements, [63]).
    - **Development Methodology**: A **purpose-driven or data-driven approach** to designing digital shadows, integrating use cases (e.g., injection molding, [64]) and refining methods for digital twin definition ([43]).
  - **Technologies for Traceability**:

    - **Conformance Relations**: Mechanisms to ensure digital shadow models adhere to reference models (e.g., Class Diagram conformance, [63]).

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from

digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital

modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods

_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].

autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.

product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_

_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
